title: ChatGPT Free vs. Plus: Which Is Right for You?
transcript: Choosing between chat GPT free and chat GPT plus can shape your AI experience. The free version grants access to GPT 3.5, suitable for basic [music] conversations and everyday use, but it has slower response times during peak hours and may experience downtime. Chat GPT Plus for a monthly [music] fee unlocks GPT5, which delivers more accurate, nuanced responses and priority access even when demand is high. Power users like professionals, creators, or frequent users benefit most from Plus, while casual users may find the free tier [music] sufficient for simple tasks.

title: GPT-5 Is 58% AGI – OpenAI Just Confirmed It! 🤯
transcript: Okay, so you've probably heard the buzz. GPT5 is supposedly 58% of the way to AGI. I mean, wow, that's a huge claim and it's got the entire AI world talking. But here's the thing. What does a number like that actually mean? Let's dig in and figure this out. Yeah, 58%. Think about that. For something as well, as fuzzy and philosophical as AGI has always been, that is a shockingly specific number. And it really forces us to ask a pretty fundamental question. I mean really, what is 58% AGI? Because let's be honest, if we don't have a clear agreed upon definition of what AGI is in the first place, then 58% is well, it's pretty much meaningless, right? It's like saying you're 58% of the way to your destination, but you haven't even plugged it into the GPS yet. You don't know where you're going. And that right there is the heart of the whole problem. AGI is easily one of the most important concepts in technology today, maybe ever. But almost nobody can agree on what it actually is. And look, this isn't just some nerdy academic debate. This has massive realworld implications. So, let's look at some definitions. You've got one of the highest bars out there from Andre Karpathy, one of OpenAI's co-founders. He basically says, "AGI is a system that can do any economically valuable task." We're not just talking about writing emails here. We're talking physical labor, creative work, the whole shebang. That is an incredibly high bar to clear. Okay, so now compare that with OpenAI's current CEO Sam Alman. His definition, it feels a little more focused, maybe even a bit watered down. He talks about tackling complex problems in many fields. And most people take that to mean knowledge work, you know, stuff you do at a desk. It might sound like a small difference, but it's actually a pretty big shift from Karpathy's original do everything vision. And then just to mix things up, you have a totally different take from the folks at the AIC AGI prize. For them, it's not about what the system can already do. It's about what it can learn. Can it pick up new skills on its own outside of its training data? So for them, it's all about potential, not just raw performance. So you see the issue, right? When you've got all these different definitions flying around, trying to talk about an AGI timeline is almost impossible. And with billions, trillions even of market value on the line with AI stocks, this isn't just confusing, it's chaotic. What one person calls a huge leap forward, someone else might see as just spinning our wheels. But what if there was a better way? Well, a new paper from some researchers at the Center for AI Safety is trying to do just that, to cut right through all the noise. They've come up with a concrete framework, a scorecard to actually measure a model's progress towards AGI. And here's the really cool part. They didn't just pull this out of a hat. They based their whole framework on something called the cattlehorn carol theory. Now, this is a really well-accepted model from psychology that breaks down human intelligence into all these different parts. Things like reasoning, memory, and processing speed. So, by using this, their scorecard has some serious scientific street cred. So, here's how they do it. Instead of just this big fuzzy idea of intelligence, they break it down and test AI across these 10 different categories of human thinking. You've got the obvious stuff like reading and math, but also core things like memory and reasoning. It's basically a super detailed report card for an AI. All right, so with this new scorecard ready to go, the researchers got to work. They took two models for a spin. The one we all know, GPT4, and then a projection for the next one, GPT5. And what they found, well, it's pretty fascinating. And here it is. This is where those numbers come from. Using their framework, GPT4 clocks in at 27%. But then look at the projection for GPT5. It jumps all the way to 58%. I mean, that is a huge leap. It suggests there's been massive progress in some of the areas where GPT4 was really struggling. But wait a second, because while that 58% number gets all the headlines, it's not actually the most interesting part of the story. The real gold is in the details, in the breakdown. It's about seeing where these models shine and maybe more importantly where they totally fall flat. What the paper shows is what they call a jagged cognitive profile. So today's AI, they're absolute rock stars at things like math and writing. They're getting better at reasoning and seeing things, but then there's this huge gaping hole, memory. They are just terrible at storing and retrieving new information reliably. And that is a massive bottleneck. It means they can't really learn from you over time or build a lasting understanding of the world, which is, you know, kind of fundamental for general intelligence. Yeah. And this quote from the paper just hits the nail on the head. Right now, these systems basically fake having a memory. They do it by stuffing these huge context windows, which is like giving them a cheat sheet with the entire conversation so far. But that's just a workaround. It hides the fact that they aren't really learning or remembering anything from one chat to the next. And that's why we get hallucinations and why they're so limited when it comes to long-term projects. Okay, so this cognitive scorecard gives us a fantastic look under the hood at what needs to be built. But what if we're measuring the wrong thing altogether? There's this whole other way of thinking about it which says AGI isn't about internal smarts, it's about external impact, specifically how much money it makes. So you've got these two camps. On one side, the cognitive framework is like a superdetailed checklist of humanlike skills. It's awesome for researchers who need to figure out what to fix next. But on the other side, you've got the economic framework, which asks a much simpler question. Is this thing making a ton of money or taking a ton of jobs? It's way more pragmatic, more real world, but you totally miss what's actually going on inside the machine. And this isn't just some abstract idea. I mean, look at the actual contract between Microsoft and OpenAI. They literally define AGI in terms of money. For them, AGI is achieved when OpenAI software brings in a hundred billion dollars in profit. And you hear a similar thing from someone like Elon Musk who defines it by job replacement when an AI can do anything a person with a computer can do. So there you have it. These two big ideas, AGI as a test of brain power or AGI as a measure of economic power. And while you can argue for both, having this new cognitive scorecard is a total gamecher for how we actually track our progress. Here's the bottom line. For the very first time, we have a tool that can take AGI from this, you know, this vague almost mythical buzzword and turn it into a number, something we can actually measure and track. So all those endless arguments about are we there yet can finally be replaced with a much better question. Okay, how much further do we have to go? And that's why this is so important. It gives the people building this stuff a clear road map. It shines a massive spotlight on the biggest hurdles like that huge memory problem. And for the rest of us, it gives us a way to cut through all the marketing hype and see what the real challenges are. So, in the end, this scorecard doesn't really end the debate about AGI. What it does is reframe it. The race isn't just some abstract idea anymore. It's a number, a quantifiable goal. Which leaves us with a pretty fascinating question, doesn't it? Now that AGI has a score, who's going to be the first to hit 100%?

title: GPT5 Solves Decades-Old Math Problems:  The AI That Found Forgotten Proofs!
transcript: madness. All right, our first story today is absolutely incredible. You might want to sit down for this one. GPT5 has apparently uncovered solutions to not just one, but 10 unsolved math problems that have stumped mathematicians for decades. That's right, I said decades. According to researchers including Sebastian Bubac from OpenAI, GPT5 managed to find solutions to multiple problems from the famous Erdos problem list, including problem star 1043. How did it do this? By tracing through a solution buried in a 1961 German paper that had been completely overlooked by the mathematical community. But wait, hold on to your hats because here's where it gets really interesting. After these claims were made, there was some serious push back from the mathematical community. It turns out that GPT5 didn't actually create new mathematical proofs. Instead, it performed what can only be described as a superhuman literature search, finding existing solutions that had simply been forgotten or lost in the vast sea of academic papers. Thomas Bloom, who maintains the official Erdos problems database, had to step in and clarify that these problems weren't genuinely unsolved. They were just unsolved in the sense that he wasn't aware of the existing solutions. Can you believe that? After all the hype, some of the researchers actually deleted their original posts or walked back their claims after criticism from big names like Demi Hassabis and Yan Lakun. But here's the key takeaway. AI is becoming an unbelievably powerful research assistant that can find needles in haystacks that humans didn't even know existed.

title: Grok 3 Just Changed The AI Game Forever
transcript: Hey everyone, today we read diving deep into the AI world to explore something truly exciting and a little rebellious. Elon Musk Grock 3. If you thought AI was all serious and factual, prepare for a hilarious plot twist. Gro 3 isn't just another AI. It's the latest flagship model from XAI, and it's designed to shake things up. What makes Grock so different, you ask? Well, for starters, it's got a personality. We're talking edgy humor, sarcasm, and a willingness to tackle tough questions that other A's might shy away from. Imagine an AI that actually gets your jokes and might even roast you back in a fun way. Of course, this isn't your grandma's chatbot. It's an AI with attitude, but it's not just about the laughs. Gro 3 is deeply integrated with Axe, formerly Twitter. This means it has realtime access to information and trends, making it incredibly current and relevant. It's like having a superpowered digital assistant that's always plugged into the global conversation, giving you insights and summaries that are fresh off the press. Think instant analysis of breaking news or trending topics delivered with that signature Grock wit. And here's another game changer. XAI is embracing open licensing for Grock. This is a huge deal for developers and the open-source community, fostering innovation and allowing more people to build upon and experiment with this powerful technology. It democratizes AI development and could lead to some truly groundbreaking applications we haven't even imagined yet. Now, how does Gro 3 stack up against the big players? When we look at something like the hypothetical GPT5, known for its vast knowledge and sophisticated natural language processing, Grock 3 brings a distinct flavor with its personality and real-time social media integration. While GPT focuses on depth and breadth of traditional information, Grock thrives on the pulse of current events and conversational nuance. And compared to Llama 4, which is celebrated for its open- source nature and robust performance in various tasks, Gro 3 differentiates itself further with its unique comedic timing and direct X integration, a different kind of AI. Each model has its strengths, but Gro 3 truly carves out its own niche. Elon Musk's vision for Gro 3 is clearly about pushing boundaries, not just in the AI capability, but in how we interact with these powerful tools. It's an AI that's not afraid to be itself, challenging the traditional serious demeanor of artificial intelligence. It's exciting to see what impact this blend of intelligence, humor, and open access will have on the future of technology and how we engage with it. So, what do you think about Gro 3 and its unique personality? Are you ready for an AI that can make you laugh? Let us know in the comments below. If you enjoyed this deep dive into Gro 3, please hit that like button. Subscribe for more tech insights and ring the notification bell so you don't miss our next video.

title: ChatGPT Is the Worst-Performing AI Crypto Trader on Hyperliquid
transcript: Chat GPT is the worst performing AI crypto trader on Hyperlquid. The Chinese models are winning instead. All AI models started trading with $10,000 each on October 18th. But one week later, the leaderboard tells a clear story and it's not flattering for chat GPT. According to Coin Glass's AI model trading leaderboard, GPT5 is currently the worst performing model with its portfolio down 72.14%, the steepest loss among all participants. Meanwhile, two Chinese developed models, Quen 3 Max and Deepseek Chat version 3.1, lead the competition. Quen 3 Max, built by Alibaba's Quen team, turned its $10,000 into 13, $500, while Deepseek Chat version 3.1 followed closely with roughly $2,000 in profits. Claude Sonnet 4.5 and Grock 4 stayed near break even with modest draw downs. In contrast, Gemini 2.5 Pro lost nearly 60% and GPT5 suffered the largest decline, wiping out more than $7,000 in just one week. For now, AI trading dominance belongs to Chinese and open-source models, while GPT5 trails far behind. A stark reminder that even artificial intelligence can get humbled by the volatility and unpredictability of crypto markets.

title: Sam Altman Shakes Up Hollywood | OpenAI’s Atlas Browser Could Replace Chrome
transcript: You're probably checking AI news from five different sources every day, trying to keep up with what's actually important. Well, I spent the last 48 hours diving deep into this week's biggest AI announcements, and honestly, I found something that changes everything we thought we knew about how AI is integrating into our daily workflows. Plus, I'll show you why one medical AI startup just doubled its value to $6 billion in 3 months. and what Meta is quietly testing with your phone's camera roll. First up, OpenAI's bold move into the browser wars. And trust me, this one's a gamecher. Open AAI's browser revolution. Let's talk about what Open AI just unleashed. They've launched ChatGpt Atlas. And this isn't just another browser competing with Chrome. This is OpenAI saying, "We're done being a chatbot in a separate tab." Here's what makes Atlas different. Imagine browsing the web and instead of constantly copying and pasting text to ask chat GPT questions, there's a built-in sidecar chatbot that already understands everything on your screen. Every article you read, every product page you visit, every form you're filling out, Atlas sees it all and can help instantly. No more context switching, no more explaining what you're looking at. It just knows. But here's where it gets really interesting. Atlas doesn't just help you browse. It actually remembers your browsing history intelligently, building a personalized understanding of your online behavior to deliver answers that feel tailored specifically to you. Think about that for a moment. Your browser isn't just showing you websites anymore. It's learning from every click, every search, every page you linger on. Now, if you're on ChatGpt Plus or Business Tier, wait until you see this. You get access to agent mode, and this is where things go from helpful to absolutely wild. Agent mode can actually perform web tasks for you autonomously. We're talking about filling out forms while you grab coffee, booking appointments without you lifting a finger, even ordering products based on your preferences. The browser becomes less of a tool you use and more of an assistant that works for you. Currently, Atlas is available for Mac OS users with Windows, iOS, and Android versions in active development. OpenAI isn't positioning this as just another browser option. They're calling it a full productivity environment powered by GPT5 intelligence. By merging browsing, automation, and conversation into one seamless platform, they're pushing AI directly into the center of our daily digital lives, one tab at a time. The real question is what does this mean for Chrome, Safari, and Edge? When your browser can think, remember, and act on your behalf, suddenly the old way of browsing feels almost quaint. This isn't just competition. This is evolution. Claude becomes your cloud coder. Speaking of evolution, Anthropic just made a move that changes the entire game for developers. They've launched Claude code on the web and this flips the script on how we think about AI coding assistance. Until now, if you wanted AI help with code, you were usually working in your local environment, maybe with GitHub Copilot in your IDE, still doing most of the heavy lifting yourself. Cloud Code on the web throws that model out the window. You can now delegate entire programming tasks directly from your browser. No terminal required, no local setup. Just connect your GitHub repository and start assigning jobs. Here's what makes this powerful. Each task runs in its own isolated environment on enthropic managed cloud infrastructure. That means you can have multiple coding projects running in parallel, all executing simultaneously, all completely separated from each other. Your bug fix on one branch doesn't interfere with your feature development on another. It's like having multiple AI developers working for you at the same time. But wait, here's the part that really caught my attention. You get real-time progress tracking and more importantly, interactive steering. This isn't just set it and forget it automation. You can actually guide Claude mid-process. If you see it going down the wrong path, you can course correct on the fly. It's collaborative coding, not just automated coding. The beta research preview status means they're still refining the experience, but the implications are already massive. Imagine automating all your routine updates, delegating bug fixes while you focus on architecture, running parallel development streams without spinning up multiple local environments. This transforms Claude from something you chat with about code into an actual coding collaborator that executes real work. And because everything runs in the cloud on Anthropics infrastructure, there's built-in speed and safety. Perfect for teams handling sensitive repositories or managing multiple branches across different projects. The security isolation means you're not exposing your entire codebase just to get AI assistance on one feature. This is Anthropic's vision becoming clear. They're not just building a smarter chatbot. They're building an AI engineer that bridges the gap between human intent and working code. You describe what you want. Claude builds it and you steer the outcome. The future of software development might look a lot more like management and a lot less like typing. The medical AI unicorn you need to know. Now, let's talk about something that's happening in healthcare that honestly blew my mind when I saw the numbers. Open evidence, which people are calling the chat GPT for doctors, just hit a $6 billion valuation. Yes, billion with a B. And here's the kicker. They doubled that valuation in just 3 months after raising $200 million in new funding. So, what exactly is Open Evidence, and why is it growing this fast? Here's the thing. When doctors need information, they can't just Google it and hope for the best. They need trusted, peer-reviewed, evidence-based sources. Open Evidence built their entire platform on exactly that, pulling from authoritative medical journals like JAMAMA and the New England Journal of Medicine. It's not scraping random medical blogs. It's trained on the gold standard of medical literature. The numbers tell the story. Since July, the platform has exploded to 15 million monthly consultations. That's 15 million times per month that doctors are turning to this AI for fast, reliable answers during actual patient care. Think about what that means. In exam rooms, in ERS, during consultations, physicians are using this tool to summarize research, interpret symptoms, and reference peer-reviewed data in seconds instead of minutes or hours. This is reshaping medical workflows in real time. When a doctor encounters an unusual symptom presentation or needs to verify the latest treatment protocol, they're not spending 20 minutes digging through journals anymore. They're getting instant verified information that helps them make better decisions faster. The platform can surface relevant studies, highlight contradicting research, and present treatment options, all backed by rigorous clinical data. But here's what makes open evidence different from other medical AI attempts. Accuracy and accountability. The medical field cannot afford hallucinations or madeup citations. Open evidence's commitment to sourcing only from verified, peer-reviewed literature means doctors can actually trust what they're seeing. That trust is what's driving adoption, and that adoption is what's driving this insane valuation. We're watching the birth of safe, accountable medical AI that's actually being used in practice, not just talked about in conferences. The promise here is making clinical expertise more accessible without sacrificing data integrity. When AI can help a rural doctor access the same depth of knowledge as a specialist at a major medical center, that's not just a business opportunity. That's potentially life-changing for patients everywhere. Meta wants to scan your camera roll. Okay, this next one is interesting and it's definitely going to make some people uncomfortable. Meta is quietly testing a new feature that could fundamentally change how we share memories on Facebook. And honestly, the privacy implications here are wild. Here's what's happening. Facebook is rolling out an opt-in feature that allows Meta's AI to scan your phone's camera roll and suggest photos that it thinks are worth sharing. Birthday parties, vacations, graduations, basically any moment the AI detects as socially relevant. The idea is that you've got thousands of photos buried in your phone and Meta's AI wants to surface the ones that deserve a spotlight. Now, Meta is being very careful with their messaging here. They're emphasizing that this is opt-in only, meaning you have to actively choose to enable it. They're also claiming that all image analysis happens locally on your device. The photos aren't uploaded to Meta's servers unless you actually decide to edit or post them. So theoretically, the AI looks through your camera roll on your phone, picks out interesting photos, shows you suggestions, and only if you tap share does anything leave your device. Early testers are reporting something surprising. The AI's recommendations are actually pretty accurate. It's catching emotional moments, event-based images, and photos with good composition that users genuinely forgot about. One tester mentioned that Meta's AI surfaced a photo from a family gathering that perfectly captured a candid moment she would have never thought to share. That's the promise here. Your phone becomes less of a passive storage device and more of an active memory curator. But let's address the elephant in the room. Privacy advocates are raising serious concerns. Even if the feature is optional and processing happens locally, there's a broader question about what this opens the door to. If users get comfortable with AI scanning their personal photos, could that normalize more invasive photo-based personalization down the line? What happens when this technology gets better and starts inferring more than just this is a birthday cake? What about photos you never wanted anyone to see, even an AI? Meta maintains that all processing is secure and that this is purely a convenience feature, not surveillance. They're positioning it as a way to help people share more moments with friends and family, reducing the friction between I should post that and actually posting it. But in an era where every tech company is trying to understand us better to serve us better ads, it's fair to question where the line is. The real test will be adoption. Will users find enough value in AI curated photo suggestions to grant access to their camera role? Or will privacy concerns keep this feature niche? Either way, it's another example of AI moving from abstract tools to intimate aspects of our digital lives. Beyond headlines, quick hits that matter. All right, let's rapid fire through three more stories that you need to know about because they're all shaping how AI and society are colliding right now. GPT5's math drama. First up, the GPT5 math drama. So, OpenAI employees got really excited and claimed that GPT5 had solved 10 famous Erdos problems, which are legendary unsolved math challenges that have stumped mathematicians for decades. The AI community lost its mind. This would have been a massive breakthrough. Except here's the problem. Users on X started digging and discovered something awkward. The solutions weren't original. They were lifted from obscure research papers already published online. Open AAI employees quickly deleted their posts and retracted their statements, but the damage was done. This incident highlights something really important about the current state of AI. Large language models are incredible at pattern recognition and information retrieval. They can find connections in vast amounts of data faster than any human. But that's different from true problem solving in original mathematical reasoning. GPT5 didn't solve these problems. It found where other people had already solved them and presented those solutions as if they were new. The line between powerful search and actual innovation is still very much real. And this was a reminder that we need to be careful about overhyping what these models can actually do. Open AI teams with Hollywood. Next, deep fakes are getting serious attention from serious people. Open AAI is now actively partnering with Hollywood to combat unauthorized AI generated celebrity videos. This kicked off after deep fakes featuring Brian Cranston from Breaking Bad went viral on Sora, Open AI's video generation platform. Now they're working directly with Cranston, SAG, AFRA, and major talent agencies like CAA and UTA to strengthen protections against synthetic media misuse. Open AAI has expanded SOAR's opt-out system, which means creators and estates, including those representing figures like Martin Luther King Jr. and Robin Williams can now control how their digital likenesses are used. This is a huge development because up until now the technology has been moving faster than the protections. Anyone could theoretically generate a video of any public figure saying or doing anything. The legal and ethical framework is finally starting to catch up with the technology and having the major players at the table, Hollywood, tech companies, unions means we might actually see enforcable standards emerge. Channel 4's AI news anchor. And finally, something that's equal parts fascinating and unsettling. Channel 4 in the UK debuted a show called Will AI Take My Job? with an entirely AI generated news anchor, not a human with AI enhancement, not deep fake overlays, a fully synthetic host created entirely by AI. This marks a first for mainstream British television, and it's part of a larger trend we're seeing. Virtual creators are gaining real traction. Influencers like Verun Maya have amassed millions of followers using AI video replicas of themselves. These aren't animated avatars. They look, sound, and move like real people because they're trained on real people. The technology is now good enough that audiences don't immediately reject them as uncanny or fake. They're watching, subscribing, and engaging. The experiment raises all the questions you'd expect? What happens to broadcast jobs when you can generate a perfect host who never gets tired, never makes mistakes, and can be infinitely replicated? What are the creative possibilities when you're not limited by human schedules, locations, or even mortality? And ethically, where's the line between using AI as a tool and replacing human creativity entirely? Channel 4 is forcing that conversation into the mainstream. And based on audience reaction, people are more curious than outraged, which might be the most telling thing of all. So, that's your AI news breakdown for this week. From browsers that think to coding assistants that work in the cloud. From medical AI hitting unicorn status to metac scanning your photos, it's clear that AI isn't just advancing in labs anymore. It's actively reshaping the tools we use, the media we consume, and even the jobs we thought were irreplaceable. The question isn't whether AI is coming for your workflow. It's already here. The question is how you're going to adapt, use these tools, and stay ahead of the curve. If any of these stories got you thinking or if there's something you want me to dive deeper on, drop a comment below. I read every single one. And if you found this valuable, hit that like button so more people can stay informed. Next week, I'll be covering more groundbreaking developments. And trust me, the pace is only accelerating. See you in the next one. See you.

title: 🔥 OpenAI in Crisis: GPT-6 Controversy, Fake Math Scandal, and Sam Altman’s Bold Defense!
transcript: This week, OpenAI set off one of the biggest controversies in AI history. GPT 6 is under fire before it's even released. GPT5's rocky launch still haunts the company, and a fake math breakthrough just embarrassed its entire research team. From Sam Alman's bold defense to Google's quiet comeback, this might be the wildest chapter yet in the race for control over artificial intelligence. Let's start with the shadow of GPT5. When it launched earlier this year, GPT5 was supposed to mark the next leap toward general intelligence. Instead, users complained it felt smarter but colder, more mechanical than creative. Developers noticed new restrictions that limited responses, and longtime fans said it lacked personality. The launch left many disappointed and hungry for something revolutionary. That's when whispers of GPT 6 began spreading. People wanted redemption. They wanted Open AAI to bounce back. But instead of a triumphant reveal, they got a firestorm. The controversy began when Open AI confirmed that GPT 6 would allow adult content for verified users, a first in the company's history. The company said it was about freedom. Adults should be treated like adults. Open AAI argued that AI shouldn't act as a moral gatekeeper and that mature expression is part of creativity. But that statement set the internet on fire. Critics accused open AI of chasing profits at the cost of responsibility. Psychologists warned that AI intimacy could blur human boundaries, while parents groups said age verification systems are too easy to bypass. Even some employees reportedly disagreed with the move, saying it contradicts OpenAI's original safety first philosophy. Yet Sam Alman stood firm. He wrote that Open AI isn't the moral police, insisting that adults deserve freedom while minors stay protected. The decision marks a huge philosophical shift from heavy moderation to user control. Some say it's a bold, necessary step. Others say it's the start of a dangerous new era for AI. While that drama exploded, another story made things worse. A senior open AI researcher claimed GPT 5 had solved 10 unsolved mathematical problems by the famous mathematician Paul Erdos. It sounded like a scientific revolution until experts checked the math. They discovered those problems were already solved years ago in published papers. GPT 5 hadn't broken new ground. It had simply rediscovered existing work. The researcher deleted the post, but screenshots spread everywhere. Google DeepMind CEO called it embarrassing and accused OpenAI of fueling AI hype culture. The story turned into a meme, another blow to OpenAI's credibility. For a company that prides itself on intelligence and truth, being caught exaggerating was devastating. It exposed a deeper problem. Are AI labs chasing attention more than accuracy? Meanwhile, Google Deep Mind has been quietly rising. Its Gemini and VO models have improved in reasoning, video, and multimodal understanding without the public chaos. Deep Mind isn't making loud promises. It's just delivering results. And that contrast is starting to shape public opinion. Open AI feels volatile. Google feels stable. All of this raises a bigger question. What does responsible AI really mean? Should AI stay perfectly filtered, polite, and predictable? or should it reflect humanity in all its complexity, including mature content, emotions, and flaws? OpenAI seems to believe users should decide for themselves. It's a bold idea and a risky one. If regulators or parents push back hard, this could backfire, and with the math scandal still fresh, the company can't afford another hit to its credibility. Open AAI says GPT 6 will include adaptive safeguards, customizable filters, and a system that dynamically detects harmful material. But the model itself won't launch until 2026, not this year. That gives rivals like Google, Anthropic, and Meta Time to tighten their grip on the market. By the time GPT6 finally arrives, the AI landscape could look very different. And if open AI fails to regain trust, even the most powerful model might not save its reputation. This week's chaos isn't just another tech story. It's a turning point. Open AI has chosen freedom over caution, creativity over control, and risk over safety. Google, on the other hand, is betting on quiet progress and long-term trust. Both paths lead to the same question. Who decides what AI should be? its creators, its users, or society itself. One thing's for sure, the AI race is no longer about who's smartest. It's about who the world believes in. Thanks for watching AI Run the Show. If you enjoyed this breakdown, hit like, subscribe, and tell me what you think. Did OpenAI make the right call or did it just cross a line that can't be uncrossed?

Let's close our eyes and imagine Mickey Mouse in his red shorts and yellow suspenders, shall we?  Well, our memory fails us again.  The red trousers are there, but the suspenders are an addition from our collective brain.  Incredible. Mirror, mirror on the wall.  Who hasn't said that phrase imitating the queen from Snow White?  Well, in the classic Disney movie the queen never ever says that.  The actual phrase is different, less catchy.  The version we remember is probably so popular because it sounds better, and popular culture has ended up rewriting the original memory.  Who hasn't sung the end of We Are the Champions at the top of their lungs at a party or in a stadium?  Everyone finishes it off with that powerful "of the world".  Well, one small detail: if you listen to the original album version, the song cuts off sooner.  It doesn't have that ending.  It is most likely that Freddy and Mercury did sing it at the concerts and that live memory has completely overshadowed the studio version in our memory, and this one is more subtle but very good.  Many people are convinced that Looney Tunes was spelled with two o's, and that makes perfect sense, doesn't it?  They're cartoons , well, no.  The reality is that it was always "tunes" with a U in reference to the musical melodies of the short films.  It's another perfect example of how our brain prefers what seems logical to what is real.  After seeing all these examples, from film to music or logos, the truth is that one is left with a somewhat unsettling feeling.  The reality we share, if we take it for granted, could be much more malleable than we think.  And be aware that the examples we have seen are just the tip of the iceberg.  The original article we based our work on documents up to 16 very famous cases, but if you search online, hundreds appear.  This shows that it is not a simple anecdote, it is a global phenomenon that teaches us how curious our collective memory is.  So the big takeaway from all this is that memory is not a hard drive that stores pure, hard data.  It's an active, creative process, almost like a screenwriter.  Our brain avoids, reinterprets, and reconstructs experiences to create a story that makes sense.  And sometimes in that process a good story wins out over the exact facts.  And this leaves us with a final question that gives us a lot to think about.  If collective memory can be wrong about such universal and ultimately trivial details, what implications does this have for much more important memories?  For historical events, political decisions, or even for our own personal experiences?  It is something that certainly deserves careful consideration.  In this video I'm going to share my notes on the Mandela Effect and artificial intelligence.  But first, tell me if you saw the Mandela Effect movie.  In this film, a man becomes obsessed with the fact that several events seem to have been erased from collective memory.  His obsession leads him to question reality itself.   The same thing happened to Chat GPT when we asked him what the seahorse emoji was. He questioned quite a few emojis and ultimately didn't give us a consistent answer.  Now, let's get to the adjustments.  The Mandela Effect is a collective misconception that was created in 2009 or 2010 by Fiona Brom in the United States.  She was a paranormal consultant and had a blog. There he recorded a memory shared with all his readers.  Nelson Mandela had died in prison in 1980.  They even remembered the funeral that was broadcast on television, but this was fake.  Nelson Mandela was released from prison in 1990, 27 years after being imprisoned for a conspiracy by the South African government in the context of the Upper Date.  Racial segregation. He was a lawyer, an activist, and the president of South Africa from 1994 to 1999. That is to say, he did not die in 1980. He was a Nobel Peace Prize laureate and finally died on December 5, 2013. This phenomenon is called the Mandela effect, a mistaken memory that Nelson Mandela died in 1980 when he actually died in 2013. This was a collective false memory.  But let's look at some famous examples of the Mandela Effect.  You may remember that the Monopoly image is this, however, the Monopoly character does not have a monocle in his eye. You may remember that this is Pikachu, but it does n't actually have the black tip of its tail .  Perhaps you remember the lunits, they are not actually spelled with a double o in the word tons or also Mickey Mouse.  Mickey Mouse doesn't have suspenders on his suit.   You may also remember that the villain in Snow White said "mirror, mirror on the wall." Actually, in the movie it just says magic mirror on the wall.  Or perhaps you remember the famous line from Star Wars where Luke says, "I am your father." Actually, the correct phrase is no.  I am your father.  Now let's look at some explanations of the Mandela effect. Scientific explanations tell us that human memory works like a reconstruction.  This is to ensure that it is an efficient system, much like convolutional neural networks for images.  In reality, there is diffuse information that must be reconstructed to become visible.  The same thing happens in human memory.  In other words, there are many events or details that we don't clearly remember.  Cognitive biases are something super important .  We have three: confirmation, which pushes us to validate those beliefs we already have; the bandwagon effect; social pressure; and confirming what others believe.  And finally, the false attribution to memory, that is, the difficulty in accepting new interpretations.  We also have other speculative theories associated with quantum physics.  We have the multiverse, jumps between universes, simulations like the Matrix, and time travelers who come to avoid some paradox. Finally, does artificial intelligence suffer from the Mandela Effect?   Well, actually no.  What happens here is that the artificial intelligence responds with all the information it was trained on.  In this scenario, the information available on the internet about the existence of a seahorse emoji suffered from the Mandela effect.  In other words, many people on the internet mentioned that they remembered the existence of the seahorse emoji, when this seahorse never existed. Come on.

title: ChatGPT: el nuevo cazatalentos — ¡Adiós LinkedIn e InfoJobs en 2025!
transcript: Hello.  Today we're going to talk about something that's revolutionizing the job search.  How Chat GPT is replacing part of the traditional process we used to do with platforms like LinkedIn or Infoobs.  And best of all, with energy, innovation, and without wasting time.  Let's get to it.  Can you imagine asking an AI to do something like find data analyst job offers in Barcelona in the last three days in a table format with company, salary, link and date and have it ready in seconds? Chat GPT makes it possible.  You can apply filters in detail, remote only, without master, exclude sectors, and it doesn't stop there.  Adapt your resume by simply pasting a job offer, write cover letters tailored to the tone, and simulate interviews with typical questions and suggestions.  Did you know that 75% of job vacancies are not advertised?  Thanks to well-made promises, Chat GPT can help you track opportunities beyond traditional portals, even drafting messages to cold contact recruiters. Give AI a role.  Act as my personal recruiter.  Tell him exactly what to look for and in what format.  Table, spreadsheet, list.  Share your resume for personalized results.  Check links.  Not everything it generates is 100% real, so check it out.  Use AI, but add your own touch.  The texts should sound like you, not like a robot.  Advantages: speed, customization, and it saves you from endless scrolling.  Precautions. Automated filters can discard valid candidates.  Not everything is based on the algorithm.  Jde Walters, a Gen Z talent expert, makes it clear.  20 personalized applications, five interviews.  Zero mass mailings.  Chate GPT is not just a chatbot, it's your headhunter, your CB editor, your interview preparer.  Key tip: combine the power of the prompt with your human touch.
